name: PyVista (VTK-based 3D Visualization)
pricing_notes: Open source (MIT); Alexander Kaszynski and contributors

performance.notes: VTK backend with OpenGL; GPU rendering; Jupyter via trame; handles millions of cells

agent_readiness.notes: High-level VTK wrapper for 3D scientific visualization. import pyvista as pv. mesh = pv.read('file.vtk'). mesh.plot(). mesh['field'] = scalar_array. pl = pv.Plotter(). pl.add_mesh(mesh, scalars='field', cmap='viridis'). pl.show(). Jupyter: pv.set_jupyter_backend('trame'). Slicing: mesh.slice(normal='x'). Contours: mesh.contour([0.5]). Glyphs, streamlines, clip. Volume rendering. Structured/unstructured grids. FEM mesh visualization. Medical imaging. GIF animation. Off-screen rendering for headless. From NumPy: pv.PolyData(points). Trame for web-based interactive viz.

gotchas: Jupyter display requires trame backend (separate install)
Headless rendering requires OSMesa or EGL setup on servers
VTK file formats (legacy .vtk vs new .vtu) can be confusing
best_when: Point cloud and mesh visualization with scalar coloring
avoid_when: Lightweight environments where VTK dependency is too heavy
Web-first 3D visualization (three.js or deck.gl more appropriate)
summary: PyVista is a high-level VTK wrapper for 3D scientific visualization. pv.read('file.vtk'). mesh['scalar'] = values; mesh.plot(scalars='scalar', cmap='viridis'). Slicing, contouring, streamlines, glyphs, volume rendering. Jupyter via trame backend. Off-screen PNG export. FEM/CFD visualization, medical imaging, point clouds. VTK backend handles millions of cells. MIT license. Use for 3D scientific data visualization; heavier than trimesh but more visualization-focused.
